nativeRange.habitat: Large lowland rivers and their floodplains; pelagic mid-water zone of the main Mekong and Chao Phraya river channels; migrates upstream to spawn during rainy season

decorPreferences: very large open swimming volume is the priority
smooth rounded substrate to protect the scaleless body
minimal sharp decor — injuries from panic-diving are common
dim lighting reduces stress and skittish behaviour
robust or absent planting (uproots plants when panicking)
tight-fitting heavy lid essential — leaps when startled

diet.notes: Juveniles are carnivorous, readily accepting sinking pellets, frozen bloodworm, earthworms, and prawns. Adults shift toward omnivory and readily take large sinking pellets, cichlid sticks, fresh vegetables, and fruit. Feed once or twice daily; substantial waste output demands a powerful filtration system. Wild fish feed on plant matter, small fish, crustaceans, and organic detritus.

goodWith: large peaceful cichlids of similar size
large peaceful tetras or similar schooling fish in public-aquarium settings only
avoidWith: any fish small enough to be swallowed
invertebrates — shrimp and snails will be eaten
slow-moving or long-finned fish that can be injured by panicking tankmates
fish requiring stable, unaggitated environments — basa panic surges injure themselves and neighbours
community aquarium fish of any kind — adult size makes cohabitation impractical outside public aquaria

imageCandidates.notes: Adult specimen showing characteristic iridescent silvery flanks and deeply forked tail. Clear image; no watermark.
FishBase species photo; verify license before use.

summary: The Iridescent Shark is a giant Mekong catfish that reaches over 1 m in the wild and is a cornerstone of Vietnamese aquaculture. Juveniles are widely sold as ornamentals but grow far too large for any home aquarium. The species is scaleless, highly skittish, and listed as Endangered by the IUCN.
careNotes:
The Basa Catfish (sold almost universally as juveniles under the name 'Iridescent Shark') is one of the most commonly mis-sold freshwater fish in the hobby. Juveniles of 5–10 cm are strikingly attractive — silver with black-edged fins and a torpedo shape — but the species is a true giant: adults in the wild regularly exceed 1 m, reaching up to 130 cm and 44 kg, and even aquarium-raised fish routinely reach 60–90 cm within 2–3 years. No standard home aquarium can house an adult humanely.

This species is not recommended for home aquaria under any circumstances, and the consensus among experienced fishkeepers and specialist publications is clear on this point. The only responsible settings are large public aquaria or specialist commercial ponds.

Scaleless body: Pangasianodon hypophthalmus is a scaleless catfish and is acutely sensitive to copper in any form. Copper-based medications (commonly used for ich/white spot), many 'multi-cure' treatments, and even trace copper from copper plumbing can be lethal. Use only copper-free treatments (e.g., fenbendazole or temperature/salt for ich).

Severely skittish: this species has extremely poor eyesight and reacts violently to sudden light changes, movement near the glass, and vibration. When startled, fish launch themselves at full speed into tank walls, lids, and equipment — causing severe lacerations, spinal damage, and death. Injuries are cumulative and common even in well-maintained aquaria. A tightly fitted heavy lid is mandatory. Dim, consistent lighting and a low-traffic location reduce (but do not eliminate) panic events.

Water quality: prodigious waste producers requiring very high-capacity mechanical and biological filtration and regular large water changes (25–50% weekly). Ammonia and nitrite spikes develop rapidly and are especially dangerous due to the scaleless skin.

IUCN status: listed as Endangered on the IUCN Red List due to overfishing, dam construction disrupting migratory spawning runs, and habitat degradation in the Mekong basin. Wild populations have declined significantly since the 1990s. The aquarium trade in juveniles draws on both wild-caught and farm-raised stock.
breedingNotes: Not bred in home aquaria. Commercial aquaculture breeding requires large outdoor ponds or raceways, hormonal induction with carp pituitary extract or HCG, and hatchery facilities. Females can produce several hundred thousand eggs per spawning. Captive breeding in a home or even most public aquarium contexts is not feasible. Wild fish undertake long upstream migrations to spawn in the upper Mekong and its tributaries during the May–June rainy season.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Pangasianodon hypophthalmus
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (to 130 cm, 44 kg), temperature range, pH, hardness, native range (Mekong and Chao Phraya basins), habitat (large rivers), diet, behaviour notes (highly skittish, vision-impaired), and clear not-recommended-for-home-aquaria advisory.

additional.name: FishBase — Pangasianodon hypophthalmus
additional.notes: Cross-checked maximum size (130 cm TL, 44 kg), taxonomy (Pangasiidae, Siluriformes), native countries (Vietnam, Thailand, Cambodia, Laos), temperature range (22–26°C), diet (omnivore shifting with age), depth range, and IUCN Endangered status.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Iridescent Shark Care Guide
additional.notes: Confirmed explicit not-recommended-for-home-aquaria advisory. Notes on juvenile mis-sale, adult size, skittish behaviour, and scaleless copper sensitivity. Tank size guidance (minimum 300-gallon cited for adults).

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Iridescent Shark
additional.notes: Featured in PFK’s 'Fish You Shouldn’t Buy' series. Emphasises the scale of mis-selling to unprepared hobbyists, trauma from panic events, and the species’ unsuitability for any home aquarium context.

additional.name: IUCN Red List — Pangasianodon hypophthalmus
additional.notes: Confirms Endangered (EN) status. Population decline driven by overfishing, Mekong dam construction blocking spawning migrations, and habitat degradation. Notes the species is a major aquaculture commodity (Vietnamese 'pangasius' or 'basa' export fish).

fish.breedingNotes: Requires hormonal induction, large outdoor ponds, and hatchery infrastructure; not achievable in home or typical public aquarium settings.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: Reaches 100+ cm at maturity; commercial food fish kept tiny in trade and outgrows all home setups within months.
